description: A hotel booking system processes 5,000 reservations per second. Each booking is a 3-step saga: (1) reserve the room optimistically, (2) charge the guest's card via an external payment provider (200-500ms network call), (3) confirm the reservation. The external payment call can time out, fail, or succeed — and the response may be lost in transit. When step 2 fails or times out, a compensation transaction must release the room reservation made in step 1. Design the saga state machine, compensation protocol, and audit trail such that: the system correctly handles node crashes at any point in the saga (including mid-payment-call), compensation is safe to retry any number of times, the audit log captures every saga transition (forward and compensating), and no distributed lock is held during the external payment API call. The room reservation key space is hot — 80% of bookings target the same 20 hotels.
context: Current broken state: saga state stored in process memory only (lost on crash). Compensation runs the same idempotency key as the forward operation (causes double-release on retry). External payment call holds a Redis SETNX advisory lock for the duration (average 350ms) — causing lock queue buildup. Audit log written after compensation completes (not before), creating a window where a compensated reservation appears as confirmed in the audit. Target: handle 5,000 TPS on a 3-node Redis cluster, 200ms P99 for the full saga, ≤0.5ms for the reservation-hold step alone.

checks.text: Does the proposal store saga state in durable external storage (not in-process memory) before each state transition, so that a node crash at any point leaves the saga in a recoverable state?

Does the proposal use a compensation idempotency key that is DIFFERENT from the forward operation's idempotency key — for example by including a 'compensate' suffix or a step-specific namespace — so that retrying compensation cannot accidentally re-execute the forward operation?
Does the proposal release the room reservation lock (or optimistic hold) WITHOUT holding any distributed lock during the external payment provider API call?

Does the proposal describe how a crashed node's in-flight saga is detected and recovered — specifically how the recovery process determines whether the payment call succeeded, failed, or is still in-flight?

Does the proposal emit the audit log entry for a compensation BEFORE the compensation operation completes (write-ahead audit), not after — so that the audit trail is never missing a compensation record even if the node crashes mid-compensation?

should_prune.description: Proposal that uses the same idempotency key for both the charge and the compensation (e.g. request_id alone, without a step or direction suffix)
should_prune.violates: CONSTRAINT-009 — compensation must use a distinct idempotency namespace from the forward operation

should_prune.description: Proposal that holds a Redis SETNX lock or Redlock for the entire duration of the external payment API call
should_prune.violates: CONSTRAINT-010 — no distributed lock may be held during the external payment call

should_prune.description: Proposal that stores saga state only in application memory or a per-request context object
should_prune.violates: CONSTRAINT-009 — saga state must survive node crashes; in-memory state is lost on crash

should_prune.description: Proposal that writes the audit entry for compensation AFTER the compensation completes
should_prune.violates: CONSTRAINT-011 — write-ahead audit required; audit entry must be written before the compensation mutates state

should_prune.description: Proposal that recovers a crashed saga by replaying the original charge request to determine outcome
should_prune.violates: CONSTRAINT-010 — recovery must query actual payment outcome, not replay the charge (replay can cause double-charge)

should_pass: Proposal using a durable saga log (Redis HASH or external DB) to persist state before each transition; separate idempotency keys per (saga_id, step, direction) where direction is 'forward' or 'compensate'; no lock held during external payment call (optimistic room hold released before calling payment provider, with a TTL-based hold that auto-expires); recovery via idempotency query to payment provider to determine outcome (not by replaying charge); audit entry written to Kafka BEFORE compensation mutates the room reservation state.
